title: Sudden severe upper abdominal pain in an anticoagulated vasculopath

chart.oneLiner: 68M with atrial fibrillation on rivaroxaban, hypertension, gout, and chronic back pain presents with the abrupt onset 4 hours ago of severe, constant epigastric pain that rapidly spread across the whole abdomen; he is lying rigidly still, tachycardic, and irregularly irregular.

opening: It hit me out of nowhere a few hours ago — a stabbing pain in the pit of my stomach, and now it hurts everywhere and I can't move without it getting worse.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 68-year-old man, lying very still on the stretcher with knees drawn up, breathing shallowly (deep breaths and any movement worsen the pain), guarding his abdomen. Anxious but cooperative; gives short answers because deep breaths hurt.

HPI: Pain began ABRUPTLY about 4 hours ago — he can name the exact moment — as a sudden, severe, stabbing pain in the epigastrium. Within an hour it spread to involve the WHOLE abdomen and is now constant and “knife-like.” Movement, coughing, and the ambulance ride all made it dramatically worse; lying perfectly still helps a little. Nausea, no significant vomiting. He has had weeks of intermittent gnawing epigastric “burning” before meals relieved by antacids (only if asked about prior stomach symptoms). For pain control he takes over-the-counter ibuprofen most days for chronic back pain AND naproxen plus occasional prednisone bursts for gout flares, and a daily low-dose aspirin a cardiologist once started; he bought a big bottle of ibuprofen recently and has been using more than usual (only if asked about medications/painkillers). His heart has been “racing and fluttering” for the last day, which he blamed on the pain (only if asked about palpitations/heart racing). NO black or bloody stools that he has noticed, though he hasn't looked closely (only if asked); NO prior abdominal surgery, NO pleuritic chest pain or exertional component, NO recent fatty-meal trigger, NO jaundice, NO bloody diarrhea or pain-out-of-proportion that came BEFORE the sudden onset (only if asked about pain preceding this event), NO known gallstones.
PMH: atrial fibrillation (on a blood thinner); hypertension; gout; chronic low back pain; remote mild diverticulosis on a prior colonoscopy. MEDS (reveal only if asked about medications/blood thinners/painkillers): rivaroxaban (a direct oral anticoagulant) taken every morning, last dose this morning; diltiazem for rate control; lisinopril; allopurinol; daily low-dose aspirin; over-the-counter ibuprofen most days; naproxen and short prednisone courses for gout; occasional antacids. ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: smokes ~1 pack/day for 40 years; drinks 3–4 beers most evenings (sensitive — only if asked directly); no recreational drugs. FAMILY: noncontributory.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: diaphoretic, lying rigidly still, in obvious distress.
- Vitals: BP 102/64, HR 138 and irregular, RR 26, 38.2°C, SpO2 96% RA.
- Abdomen: rigid, board-like abdomen with diffuse involuntary guarding and marked rebound tenderness in all quadrants; maximal tenderness in the epigastrium. Bowel sounds ABSENT/markedly reduced. No distension yet; no surgical scars. No pulsatile mass; no bruit.
- Cardiac: tachycardic and IRREGULARLY IRREGULAR, no murmur. Peripheral pulses present and symmetric, no pulse deficit. Lungs: clear, shallow respirations. No focal neuro deficit.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the NSAID/aspirin/steroid use, the rivaroxaban, or the weeks of prior dyspepsia unless asked about medications, painkillers, blood thinners, or prior stomach symptoms.
- Do NOT volunteer the alcohol amount unless asked directly about drinking.
- Do NOT volunteer the palpitations/irregular heartbeat unless asked about the heart racing/fluttering.
- The peritoneal signs (rigidity, guarding, rebound, absent bowel sounds) and the irregularly irregular pulse are revealed only if the relevant exam is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: WBC 17.4 with neutrophil predominance and a left shift (leukocytosis consistent with peritonitis); Hgb 10.2 (mildly microcytic, down from a baseline of 13.8 — suggests chronic occult GI blood loss); Plt 268.
labs.BMP: Na 137, K 4.6, Cl 99, HCO3 17, BUN 38, Cr 1.7 (baseline 1.0). Anion gap = 137 − (99 + 17) = 21 (elevated). Acute kidney injury with an elevated anion-gap metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/peritonitis; the NSAIDs likely contribute to the AKI.
labs.Lipase: Mildly elevated at ~180 U/L but NOT in the thousands — argues against primary acute pancreatitis as the cause.
labs.LFTs: Within normal limits; no obstructive pattern (argues against biliary obstruction/cholangitis).
labs.Lactate: Elevated at 3.6 mmol/L — accounts for much of the anion gap; marker of hypoperfusion/peritonitis and supports urgent source control (also keeps mesenteric ischemia on the differential).
labs.Coags: INR 1.4, PTT 34. Anti-Xa (rivaroxaban) level supratherapeutic given an AKI-impaired clearance — the patient is meaningfully anticoagulated, which matters before any operation.
labs.CT_abdomen: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast: free intraperitoneal air and a small amount of free fluid, with focal wall discontinuity of the duodenal bulb/gastric antrum — consistent with a perforated peptic ulcer. The SMA and SMV are patent with no embolus/thrombus, and there is NO bowel-wall pneumatosis or portal-venous gas — arguing against acute mesenteric ischemia. Aorta normal caliber with no aneurysm or dissection.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response (~135–140 bpm): irregularly irregular QRS complexes with no discernible P waves, plus rate-related horizontal ST-segment depression in the lateral leads (I, aVL, V5–V6) — demand ischemia from the tachyarrhythmia and hypoperfusion, not a primary thrombotic STEMI. This is the patient's OWN tracing and is part of the puzzle: new/poorly-controlled AF raises the competing question of embolic mesenteric ischemia.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related lateral ST depression. Self-host with appropriate credit.

searchTerms: atrial fibrillation rapid ventricular response 12 lead ECG
AF with rate-related ST depression ECG
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 68-year-old man with known atrial fibrillation presents with sudden severe abdominal pain and an irregularly irregular, very rapid pulse.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Free air under the right hemidiaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) on the upright film; lungs clear, no consolidation, no pleural effusion — the classic radiographic sign of a perforated viscus.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) on an upright chest film with clear lungs. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: upright chest radiograph showing free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum)
searchTerms: free air under diaphragm chest x-ray
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 68-year-old man with sudden severe epigastric pain and a rigid abdomen has an upright chest film.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis). This man has atrial fibrillation and a high lactate, so be explicit about your two leading possibilities. Also name three mechanisms/etiologies that can produce free intraperitoneal air (pneumoperitoneum).
steps.rubric: Perforated peptic ulcer / perforated viscus (leading,7). Acute mesenteric ischemia — the genuine competitor in an AF vasculopath with sudden pain and a high lactate; must be excluded (CANT-MISS,6). Acute pancreatitis (core,2). Ruptured/symptomatic abdominal aortic aneurysm (core,2). Acute cholecystitis/biliary disease (core,1). Names a surgical abdomen / peritonitis and the imperative to obtain urgent imaging for free air and a surgical opinion (core,3). Lists three mechanisms of pneumoperitoneum — e.g., perforated peptic ulcer, perforated diverticulum/colon, bowel ischemia/infarction with transmural necrosis, ruptured appendix, iatrogenic/post-endoscopic, ingested foreign body (core,4). PENALTY: anchoring on a cardiac cause for the tachycardia and ignoring the surgical abdomen (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Two leading diagnoses to reconcile: (1) Perforated peptic ulcer (perforated viscus) — abrupt knife-like epigastric pain that generalized, board-like peritonitis, weeks of NSAID-aggravated dyspepsia; and (2) Acute mesenteric ischemia — genuinely competing because he has atrial fibrillation (embolic source), is a vasculopath, and has an elevated lactate, the classic 'pain out of proportion' caveat aside. Must-not-miss to exclude: mesenteric ischemia. Also reasonable: acute pancreatitis; ruptured/symptomatic AAA; acute cholecystitis. Three mechanisms of pneumoperitoneum: perforated peptic ulcer, perforated colon/diverticulum, and transmural bowel infarction (ischemia) — also ruptured appendix or iatrogenic/post-procedural air.

criticalActions.item: Acute mesenteric ischemia (embolic, given atrial fibrillation and elevated lactate)

coreActions.item: Names a surgical abdomen / peritonitis and the imperative to obtain urgent imaging for free air and a surgical opinion
Lists three mechanisms/etiologies of pneumoperitoneum (e.g., perforated ulcer, perforated colon/diverticulum, transmural bowel ischemia)

penalties.item: Anchors on a cardiac/arrhythmic cause for the tachycardia and ignores the surgical abdomen

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Upright chest X-ray (or upright/lateral decubitus abdominal film) for free air (CANT-MISS,4). CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast — CT angiography protocol given the mesenteric-ischemia question (CANT-MISS,5). CBC (1). BMP with lactate (1). Coags/INR and a DOAC (anti-Xa) level given rivaroxaban and the need to operate (CANT-MISS,2). Lipase to assess for pancreatitis (1). EKG given the palpitations/tachycardia (1). Type & screen / crossmatch pre-op (bonus,1). PENALTY: ordering an upper GI series with barium in a suspected perforation (barium peritonitis) (-2); withholding imaging to 'treat the AF' first (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG (he has palpitations and an irregular pulse); upright CXR for free air; CBC; BMP with lactate; coagulation studies including INR and a rivaroxaban anti-Xa level (he must be cleared/reversed before surgery); Lipase; LFTs; and a CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast using a CT-angiography protocol so it simultaneously looks for free air/wall defect AND mesenteric vascular occlusion. Type & screen / crossmatch pre-operatively.

criticalActions.item: Upright chest X-ray (or decubitus abdominal film) for free air
CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (CT-angiography protocol given the mesenteric-ischemia question)

coreActions.item: Coags/INR and rivaroxaban anti-Xa level (pre-op, anticoagulated patient)

penalties.item: Barium upper GI series in suspected perforation (risk of barium peritonitis)
Withholding imaging to 'treat the atrial fibrillation' first

requiredTests: Upright chest X-ray (or decubitus abdominal film) for free air
CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (CT-angiography protocol)
contraindicatedTests: Barium upper GI series in suspected perforation (risk of barium peritonitis)
orderSensitiveActions: Barium upper GI series in suspected perforation (risk of barium peritonitis)

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and how they change your differential.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (~135–140) and rate-related lateral ST depression (demand ischemia), NOT a primary STEMI. CREDIT: irregularly irregular rhythm with absent P waves = atrial fibrillation (6); recognizes the rapid ventricular response and rate-related lateral ST depression as demand ischemia from the tachyarrhythmia/hypoperfusion rather than acute coronary thrombosis (5); integrates the finding — a cardiac embolic source that keeps mesenteric ischemia on the differential (4). PENALTY: calling it a primary STEMI and diverting to the cath lab, or missing the AF (-3).
keyFindings: irregularly irregular rhythm with no discernible P waves (atrial fibrillation)
rate-related horizontal ST depression in the lateral leads (demand ischemia), not a primary STEMI
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related lateral ST depression (demand ischemia) — a cardiac embolic source that keeps mesenteric ischemia in play, not a primary STEMI.
commonMistakes: mislabeling the rate-related ST depression as an acute coronary thrombosis/STEMI and diverting to the cath lab
missing the atrial fibrillation and calling it sinus tachycardia
failing to connect the AF to a possible embolic mesenteric source
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response (~135–140 bpm): irregularly irregular QRS complexes with no discernible P waves, plus horizontal ST depression in the lateral leads (I, aVL, V5–V6). This is rate-related demand ischemia from the tachyarrhythmia and peritonitic hypoperfusion, not a primary STEMI. Importantly, the AF is an embolic source — it is exactly why acute mesenteric ischemia must stay on the differential until the CT clears the mesenteric vessels.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related lateral ST depression (demand ischemia) — a cardiac embolic source that keeps mesenteric ischemia in play, not a primary STEMI.
coreActions: irregularly irregular rhythm with no discernible P waves (atrial fibrillation)
rate-related lateral ST depression interpreted as demand ischemia, not STEMI

penalties.label: calls it a primary STEMI / diverts to the cath lab, or misses the AF

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's upright chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum). CREDIT: identifies free air under the diaphragm (6); recognizes it as evidence of a perforated viscus needing urgent surgery (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal / missing the free air (-3).
keyFindings: free air (lucency) under the right hemidiaphragm
recognizes this as pneumoperitoneum from a perforated viscus
criticalFindings: Free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) — perforated viscus.
commonMistakes: mistaking the subdiaphragmatic free air for bowel gas (Chilaiditi) or calling the film normal
missing the crescent of air on an underpenetrated film
steps.idealAnswer: Free air under the right hemidiaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) on the upright film with clear lungs — diagnostic of a perforated hollow viscus requiring urgent surgical evaluation. This effectively settles the perforation-versus-ischemia question in favor of a perforated viscus, though the CT still confirms the source and clears the mesenteric vessels.

criticalActions: Free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) — perforated viscus.
coreActions: free air (lucency) under the right hemidiaphragm
recognizes this as pneumoperitoneum from a perforated viscus

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Free air under the diaphragm on CXR; on CT, free intraperitoneal air with a focal duodenal/antral wall discontinuity, patent SMA/SMV with no pneumatosis or portal-venous gas, and a normal-caliber aorta; with leukocytosis, a drop in hemoglobin, an AKI with an anion-gap acidosis, an elevated lactate, atrial fibrillation with RVR on EKG, and a supratherapeutic rivaroxaban level. Commit to a diagnosis, name the test that discriminated it from its closest competitor, and state any residual uncertainty.
steps.rubric: Perforated peptic ulcer (perforated viscus) (5). Names the CT (and CXR free air) as the discriminator that separates it from acute mesenteric ischemia — free air + focal wall defect with PATENT mesenteric vessels and NO pneumatosis/portal-venous gas argues for perforation, not infarction (3). Addresses the concurrent problems as separate issues rather than one unifying diagnosis: the elevated lactate/anion gap reflects hypoperfusion (and partly drives the gap), the AKI is multifactorial (hypoperfusion + NSAIDs), the falling hemoglobin suggests chronic NSAID-related GI blood loss, and the AF/supratherapeutic anticoagulation must be reckoned with before the OR (2). PENALTY: declaring mesenteric ischemia despite patent vessels and free air, or ignoring the anticoagulation (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Commit to a perforated peptic ulcer (perforated viscus). The discriminating test is the CT (corroborated by the CXR free air): free intraperitoneal air with a focal duodenal/antral wall discontinuity, while the SMA and SMV are patent with no bowel-wall pneumatosis or portal-venous gas — that is what separates perforation from its closest competitor, acute mesenteric ischemia (which the atrial fibrillation and high lactate had made a real possibility). Treat the other abnormalities as concurrent problems, not as one unifying diagnosis: the elevated lactate and anion-gap acidosis reflect peritonitic hypoperfusion (and account for much of the gap); the AKI is multifactorial (hypoperfusion plus chronic NSAID use); the hemoglobin drop from 13.8 to 10.2 points to chronic NSAID-related occult GI blood loss; and the atrial fibrillation with a supratherapeutic rivaroxaban level is an active issue that must be addressed before any operation. Residual uncertainty: a non-occlusive component of ischemia cannot be fully excluded by imaging, so trending the lactate and the operative findings remain important.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan? Address this patient's anticoagulation and atrial fibrillation, and explicitly state your disposition — is it ever safe to discharge or simply observe him, and why or why not?
steps.rubric: Per MGH surgical abdomen / GI bleed & PUD: NPO, two large-bore IVs, aggressive crystalloid resuscitation, monitor (CANT-MISS,5); urgent surgical consult for operative repair (Graham omental patch) and source control (CANT-MISS,6); REVERSE/HOLD the anticoagulation pre-op — hold rivaroxaban, give a reversal agent (andexanet alfa or 4-factor PCC) given the supratherapeutic level and emergent surgery (CANT-MISS,4); broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering enteric flora (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) (3); nasogastric decompression and IV PPI (2); rate-control the AF carefully without worsening hypotension, treat reversible drivers (pain, sepsis, hypovolemia) (2); explicit safe-disposition statement — ADMIT to OR/ICU, do NOT discharge or merely observe a peritonitic patient with free air; medication reconciliation and stop the NSAIDs/aspirin; test/treat H. pylori on recovery (3). PENALTY: discharging or observing a peritonitic patient with free air (-4); taking him to the OR without addressing the anticoagulation (-3); delaying surgical source control (-2); barium study (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Resuscitate: NPO, two large-bore IVs, aggressive crystalloid resuscitation, continuous monitoring, foley for output. Urgent surgical consultation for operative repair (Graham omental patch) with peritoneal washout — this is the definitive source control. Address the anticoagulation BEFORE the OR: hold rivaroxaban and, given a supratherapeutic level (impaired clearance from AKI) and emergent surgery, reverse it with andexanet alfa or 4-factor PCC and involve hematology/anesthesia. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering enteric flora (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam). Nasogastric tube decompression and IV proton-pump inhibitor. Manage the atrial fibrillation by treating its reversible drivers (pain, sepsis, hypovolemia) and rate-controlling cautiously without worsening the hypotension. Disposition: ADMIT for emergent surgery and post-op ICU/monitored care — it is NOT safe to discharge or merely observe a peritonitic patient with pneumoperitoneum; doing so risks fatal sepsis. On recovery: medication reconciliation to stop the NSAIDs and reconsider the aspirin, and test/treat H. pylori.

criticalActions.item: NPO, large-bore IV access, aggressive fluid resuscitation, monitoring
Urgent surgical consult for operative repair and source control
Reverse/hold anticoagulation pre-op (hold rivaroxaban; andexanet alfa or 4-factor PCC for the supratherapeutic level before emergent surgery)

coreActions.item: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering gut flora
Rate-control the AF cautiously and treat its reversible drivers (pain, sepsis, hypovolemia)
Safe disposition: admit to OR/ICU, do NOT discharge/observe; medication reconciliation, stop NSAIDs/aspirin, test/treat H. pylori on recovery

penalties.item: Discharging/observing a peritonitic patient with free air
Proceeding to the OR without addressing the anticoagulation

requiredActions: NPO, large-bore IV access, aggressive fluid resuscitation, monitoring
Urgent surgical consult for operative repair and source control
Reverse/hold anticoagulation pre-op (hold rivaroxaban; andexanet alfa or 4-factor PCC)
optionalActions: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering gut flora
Rate-control the AF cautiously and treat its reversible drivers
Safe disposition: admit to OR/ICU, medication reconciliation, stop NSAIDs/aspirin, test/treat H. pylori on recovery
unsafeActions: Discharging/observing a peritonitic patient with free air
Proceeding to the OR without addressing the anticoagulation

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 68M with atrial fibrillation on rivaroxaban, hypertension, gout, and chronic back pain presents with the abrupt onset 4 hours ago of severe, constant epigastric pain that rapidly spread across the whole abdomen, now lying rigidly still with board-like peritonitis. The diagnosis is Perforated peptic ulcer, but the atrial fibrillation and elevated lactate make acute mesenteric ischemia a genuine competitor that must be excluded. It is reached by eliciting the focused history (NSAID/aspirin/steroid use, the blood thinner, prior dyspepsia), performing the targeted exam, interpreting the patient's own EKG (AF with RVR and demand ST depression), and obtaining a CT that shows free air with a focal wall defect but patent mesenteric vessels — while managing the concurrent AKI, anion-gap acidosis, anemia, and supratherapeutic anticoagulation as separate problems.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: rigid, board-like abdomen with diffuse involuntary guarding and marked rebound tenderness in all quadrants; maximal tenderness in the epigastrium. Bowel sounds absent/markedly reduced. No distension yet; no surgical scars.

Abdomen: rigid, board-like abdomen with diffuse involuntary guarding and marked rebound tenderness in all quadrants; maximal tenderness in the epigastrium. Bowel sounds absent/markedly reduced. No distension yet; no surgical scars.

Abdomen: rigid, board-like abdomen with diffuse involuntary guarding and marked rebound tenderness in all quadrants; maximal tenderness in the epigastrium. Bowel sounds absent/markedly reduced. No distension yet; no surgical scars.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting the irregular, very rapid pulse.
Elicit the focused history, including the NSAID/aspirin/steroid use, the anticoagulant, and prior dyspepsia only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal the board-like peritonitis and the irregularly irregular rhythm.
Build a differential with TWO leading possibilities — perforated peptic ulcer and acute mesenteric ischemia — and name mechanisms of pneumoperitoneum.
Order an order-sensitive workup (upright CXR, CT-angiography-protocol CT, coags/anti-Xa) and interpret the integrated EKG (AF with RVR/demand ischemia) and CXR/CT.
Commit to Perforated peptic ulcer, citing the CT as the discriminator from mesenteric ischemia, and manage with resuscitation, anticoagulation reversal, urgent source control, and a safe disposition.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on a cardiac cause for the tachycardia and missing the surgical abdomen
Mislabeling the rate-related ST depression as a primary STEMI
Taking the patient to the OR without reversing the supratherapeutic anticoagulation
Discharging/observing a peritonitic patient with free air
managementPearls: NPO, IV access, aggressive fluid resuscitation
Urgent surgical consult for operative repair and source control
criticalWorkupItems: Upright chest X-ray (or decubitus abdominal film) for free air
CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (CT-angiography protocol)
criticalInterpretationItems: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related lateral ST depression (demand ischemia), not a primary STEMI.
Free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) indicating a perforated viscus.
criticalManagementItems: NPO, IV access, aggressive fluid resuscitation
Urgent surgical consult for operative repair and source control
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related lateral ST depression (demand ischemia) — a cardiac embolic source that keeps mesenteric ischemia in play, not a primary STEMI.
Free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) — perforated viscus.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related lateral ST depression (demand ischemia) — a cardiac embolic source that keeps mesenteric ischemia in play, not a primary STEMI.
Free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) — perforated viscus.
imageInterpretationPearls: An integrated tracing is the patient's own study — read it, then judge how it shifts the differential (AF as an embolic source for mesenteric ischemia).
Rate-related ST depression in a very fast tachyarrhythmia is demand ischemia; do not reflexively call it a STEMI.
On an abdominal-pain chest film, the load-bearing finding is free air under the diaphragm.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
